section.title: Our Eyes Look to the Lord
section.sectionRationale: The fourth of the fifteen Songs of Ascents, Psalms 120 to 134, sung by pilgrims going up to Jerusalem, placed in Book V of the Psalter, Psalms 107 to 150. Even having arrived at God's city in Psalm 122, His people still need mercy, and this short psalm turns their eyes upward in humble dependence.
section.thesis: Like a servant's eyes fixed on a master's hand, the psalmist's eyes look steadily to the LORD enthroned in heaven, pleading for mercy amid the contempt and scorn of the proud.
section.frameNote: Two movements: eyes lifted to the enthroned LORD in servant like dependence, verses 1 to 2, and the repeated plea for mercy amid endured contempt, verses 3 to 4.

units.label: I lift up my eyes to You

verses.text: I lift up my eyes to You, the One enthroned in heaven.
As the eyes of servants look to the hand of their master, as the eyes of a maidservant look to the hand of her mistress, so our eyes are on the LORD our God until He shows us {{chanan|mercy}}.

units.label: Have mercy on us, O Lord

verses.text: Have mercy on us, O LORD, have mercy, for we have endured much {{buz|contempt}}.
We have endured much scorn from the arrogant, much contempt from the proud.

chanan.gloss: To show favor, grace, or mercy. The psalmist's eyes wait on the LORD precisely for this undeserved kindness, as a servant waits on a master's hand for provision.

buz.gloss: Contempt, scorn, disdain. Doubled with a related word for scorn in verse 4, it describes the weight of mockery the psalmist and his people have endured from the arrogant.

basic.context: This short Song of Ascents shows that even God's gathered people, having just arrived in Jerusalem in Psalm 122, still live under mockery and contempt from the proud, and so still need mercy, turning their eyes to the LORD as a servant watches a master's hand.
basic.hermeneutics: It describes the real experience of enduring scorn from the arrogant, whether in exile, under foreign rule, or simply among the proud of any age, and prescribes as an abiding posture fixed, patient dependence on the LORD for mercy.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalmist pleads for chanan, undeserved favor and mercy, using the doubled buz, contempt, to underline how much scorn from the proud his people have borne, all while their eyes stay fixed on the LORD's hand like a servant's.
basic.typology: The servant's watchful eyes on the master's hand anticipate Christ, who took on the very form of a servant, and who Himself endured the scorn of the proud and arrogant at His trial and on the cross.
basic.christ: Jesus, taking the form of a servant, Philippians 2:7, endured far greater scorn from the arrogant than this psalmist, Hebrews 12:3, and yet fixed His eyes on the Father, modeling the very dependence this psalm describes.
basic.god: God is enthroned in heaven, sovereign over all, yet attentive to the watchful eyes of His servants, ready to show mercy to those who endure contempt while waiting on Him rather than retaliating.
basic.crossReferences: Philippians 2:7 shows Christ taking the form of a servant. Hebrews 12:2-3 calls believers to fix their eyes on Jesus who endured hostility from sinners. Luke 18:13-14 shows the tax collector pleading for mercy, echoing this psalm's cry.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's servant like dependence on the LORD's mercy amid scorn finds its perfect pattern and its answer in Christ, the true Servant, who endured contempt yet remained fixed on the Father and now shows mercy to all who look to Him.

context.literary: A short communal lament within the Songs of Ascents, moving from individual confession, 'I lift up my eyes,' to communal plea, 'our eyes,' 'have mercy on us,' within just four verses.
context.historical: Fits any period when Israel endured mockery from surrounding, more powerful peoples, whether the taunts of hostile neighbors in the time of the judges and kings or the scorn faced by the post exilic community rebuilding under foreign rule.
context.canonical: Follows Psalm 122's joyful arrival at Jerusalem with a sober reminder that even in God's city, His people still need His mercy amid an often hostile world.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To give voice to a community enduring contempt from the powerful and proud, teaching them to fix their eyes on the LORD in humble, patient dependence rather than despair or retaliation.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific experience of scorn from the arrogant, likely under foreign or hostile pressure. It prescribes as an abiding pattern turning to the LORD in servant like dependence whenever contempt is endured.

debates.question: Is this psalm's setting pre-exilic or post-exilic Israel?

views.label: Pre-exilic, reflecting hostility from neighboring peoples
views.case: Israel faced mockery from surrounding nations throughout the period of the judges and the monarchy, such as Goliath's taunts, fitting this psalm's language of scorn from the arrogant.

views.label: Post-exilic, reflecting contempt under foreign rule or from hostile neighbors like Sanballat
views.case: The specific complaint of enduring 'much contempt' fits the documented ridicule the returned exiles faced while rebuilding, as recorded in Nehemiah 4.

debates.bearing: Either setting yields the same pastoral point: God's people in every age have needed to turn contemptuous treatment into occasion for humble dependence on the LORD's mercy.

study.originalLanguages: The psalmist pleads for chanan, undeserved favor, using doubled language of buz, contempt, to describe scorn borne from the proud, all while keeping eyes fixed on the LORD's hand exactly as a servant watches a master's.

typology.type: The servant's eyes fixed on the master's hand, verse 2
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who took the very form of a servant, Philippians 2:7, and fixed His eyes on the Father's will even to the point of death.

typology.type: Enduring contempt and scorn from the proud, verses 3-4
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, who endured such hostility from sinners against Himself, Hebrews 12:3, and mercifully prayed for those who mocked Him, Luke 23:34.

study.christ: Jesus, taking the very form of a servant, Philippians 2:7, endured scorn from the arrogant far exceeding what this psalm describes, mocked at His trial and on the cross, Matthew 27:29-31, yet He fixed His eyes on the Father throughout, John 5:19, and even prayed mercy for His mockers, Luke 23:34, embodying and answering this psalm's cry.
study.god: God is enthroned in heaven, sovereign over all, yet attentive to His servants' watchful eyes, ready to show mercy to those who endure contempt while waiting patiently on Him rather than repaying scorn with scorn.

crossReferences.note: Christ took the very form of a servant, embodying the servant like posture this psalm describes toward the LORD.
Calls believers to fix their eyes on Jesus, who endured hostility from sinners, echoing this psalm's steady gaze amid scorn.
The tax collector's plea for mercy echoes this psalm's repeated cry, 'have mercy on us, O LORD, have mercy.'

study.oneStory: This psalm's servant like dependence on the LORD's mercy amid scorn finds its perfect pattern and its answer in Christ, the true Servant, who endured contempt yet remained fixed on the Father and now shows mercy to all who lift their eyes to Him.

sermon.title: Three Songs of Faith
sermon.why: Begg preaches Psalms 123 to 125 together, drawing out this psalm's picture of servant like eyes fixed on the LORD amid contempt as a pattern of faith for enduring scorn while waiting on God's mercy.

quiz.q: To what does the psalmist compare his eyes looking to the LORD?

options.text: A servant's eyes on the hand of a master, or a maidservant's on her mistress.
options.note: Verse 2 gives this exact double comparison.

options.text: A child's eyes on a distant star.
options.note: The comparison is specifically to servants watching a master's hand, not stargazing.

options.text: A soldier's eyes on the enemy line.
options.note: The image is one of humble dependence, not military watchfulness.

options.text: A farmer's eyes on the sky for rain.
options.note: That image appears in psalms about harvest, not this servant comparison.

quiz.q: What does the psalmist repeatedly ask the LORD for in verse 3?

options.text: Mercy, repeated for emphasis.
options.note: 'Have mercy on us, O LORD, have mercy' is stated twice for urgency.

options.text: Wealth and material increase.
options.note: The plea is specifically for mercy, not riches.

options.text: A new king to be appointed.
options.note: No request for a new ruler appears in this psalm.

options.text: Victory in battle.
options.note: The psalm addresses scorn and contempt, not a military campaign.

quiz.q: What has the psalmist's community endured, according to verses 3-4?

options.text: Much contempt and scorn from the arrogant and proud.
options.note: This is stated twice for emphasis across both verses.

options.text: A severe famine.
options.note: The complaint is about mockery, not hunger.

options.text: Loss of their homeland entirely.
options.note: The psalm's specific complaint is social scorn, not the loss of the land itself.

options.text: Betrayal by close family members.
options.note: The scorn comes from 'the arrogant' and 'the proud,' not specifically family.

quiz.q: Where is the LORD described as being enthroned, in verse 1?

options.text: In heaven.
options.note: The psalmist lifts his eyes to the One enthroned in heaven.

options.text: On Mount Zion alone.
options.note: Zion is significant elsewhere in the Ascents, but this verse specifically names heaven.

options.text: In the wilderness tabernacle only.
options.note: The text points to heaven, not a single earthly location.

options.text: Among the stars of the night sky.
options.note: The text says heaven broadly, not a specific astronomical image.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's servant posture connect to Christ?

options.text: Christ took the very form of a servant and endured scorn from the arrogant, yet kept His eyes fixed on the Father.
options.note: Philippians 2:7 and Hebrews 12:2-3 show this fulfillment.

options.text: It has no meaningful connection to the New Testament.
options.note: The servant imagery and endurance of contempt directly foreshadow Christ's own experience.

options.text: It shows Christ demanding honor rather than enduring scorn.
options.note: The Gospels show Christ enduring mockery humbly, not demanding honor for Himself.

options.text: It predicts the specific number of years until Christ's coming.
options.note: The connection is one of pattern and fulfillment, not a numerical prediction.

questions: The psalmist compares his dependence on God to a servant watching a master's hand. What would it look like this week to wait on God that attentively, rather than acting first on your own?

This psalm names real contempt and scorn endured from the proud. Where have you experienced that kind of mockery, and how does bringing it honestly to God, as this psalm does, change how you carry it?
The psalmist repeats his plea, 'have mercy on us, O LORD, have mercy.' Is there a need in your life right now urgent enough to warrant that kind of repeated, earnest prayer?
Christ took the very form of a servant and endured contempt far greater than this psalmist's. How does His example shape the way you respond when you are looked down on or dismissed?
This psalm moves from an individual 'I' in verse 1 to a communal 'we' and 'us' in verses 2 through 4. Who in your community needs you to lift your eyes to the LORD alongside them right now?

translationNotes.note: The double comparison, a servant to a master and a maidservant to a mistress, intensifies the picture of complete, watchful dependence rather than adding a separate example.
The repetition 'have mercy on us, O LORD, have mercy' is a Hebrew intensifying device expressing the depth and urgency of the plea, not mere redundancy.
Contempt and scorn in Hebrew describe not mild disrespect but active, disdainful mockery, the kind that wounds through ridicule rather than physical harm.

meta.notes: BSB text taken verbatim from biblehub.com's BSB page for Psalm 123 (the local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error, so the web fallback was used for all of Psalms 120-132). Verse count (4) and wording cross-checked against the section map bounds Psalms 123:1-4. No em or en dashes present. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming Alistair Begg's sermon 'Three Songs of Faith' (covering Psalms 123-125) at truthforlife.org.
